content:
# Transmission Amplitude Variation with Offset (TAVO)

**Summary**: The thesis investigates a new method called Transmission Amplitude Variation with Offset (TAVO) to estimate elastic parameters from VSP data. It proposes simplified linear and polynomial approximations for transmission coefficients (TPP and TPS) based on Aki and Richards equations, demonstrating their effectiveness in estimating $\beta/\alpha$, $\Delta\beta/\beta$, and $\Delta\rho/\rho$ across interfaces.

---

## Research Problem

Existing AVO methods using reflected waves are limited in their ability to accurately estimate certain elastic parameters like density contrast (∆ρ/ρ) due to high sensitivity to regression coefficient errors. The study addresses this by exploring transmitted wave modes (TAVO) and linearizing the Zoeppritz equations for better estimation of $\beta/\alpha$ and $\Delta\beta/\beta$.

## Objectives

- Derive simplified approximations for transmission P-wave (TPP) and S-wave (TPS) coefficients based on Aki and Richards equations.
- Evaluate the accuracy of these new approximations compared to original Aki and Richards formulations using three distinct reservoir models.
- Develop a method to invert TAVO data into elastic parameter contrasts ($\beta/\alpha$, $\Delta\beta/\beta$, and $\Delta\rho/\rho$).
- Derive approximations for transmission amplitude versus offset from Aki and Richards (2002) equations.
- Linearize transmitted wave modes to facilitate the estimation of relative changes in elastic properties through linear regression analysis.
- Evaluate the sensitivity and error propagation of different models (Model 1, 2, and 3) in estimating $\beta/\alpha$, $\Delta\beta/\beta$, and $\Delta\rho/\rho$.

## Methods

The study utilizes mathematical modeling to derive linear approximations for TPP (Equation 3.5) and polynomial expansions for TPS (Equation 3.9). These are compared against the original Aki and Richards equations. Synthetic data is generated using a forward modeling approach where amplitudes are convolved with a 40-Hz zero-phase Ricker wavelet. A regression analysis (least squares) is then used to invert these amplitudes into elastic parameters. The study also includes an error propagation analysis to quantify how uncertainties in the regression coefficients affect the final parameter estimates.

## Data and Materials

The study uses three synthetic reservoir models: Model 1 (oil-sand), Model 2 (gas-sand), and Model 3 (gas-sand from Gulf of Mexico). Synthetic data is generated using 31 shot points at intervals of 164 ft, with a receiver located at 4937 ft depth.

## Key Findings

- The TPP approximation (Equation 3.4) showed an error of less than 0.001% compared to the Aki and Richards equations across all models. ([[2014-kola|Kola, 2014, p. 57]])
- The 3-term TPS approximation (Equation 3.8) yielded errors under 10% for angles up to 50 degrees in Model 1, and remained accurate enough for practical exploration cases where incidence angles rarely exceed 45 degrees. ([[2014-kola|Kola, 2014, p. 57]])
- Estimation of $\Delta\rho/\rho$ using TAVO showed high error across all models at higher angles due to the non-linearity of the specific equation relative to the regression coefficients. ([[2014-kola|Kola, 2014, p. 53]])
- Model 1 (oil sand) and Model 2 (gas sand) showed distinct TPP responses, while both gas-sand models (1 and 3) exhibited similar TPP trends. ([[2014-kola|Kola, 2014, p. 14]])
- The estimation of $\beta/\alpha$ using the 3-term TPS approximation was successful for all three models, with values close to true parameters (e.g., Model 2: True 0.5239 vs Estimated 0.5679). ([[2014-kola|Kola, 2014, p. 52]])
- Estimation of $\beta/\alpha$ and $\Delta\beta/\beta$ was found to be more robust than $\Delta\rho/\rho$, which showed high sensitivity to small perturbations in regression coefficients C, D, and E. ([[2014-kola|Kola, 2014, p. 77]])
- Model 2 showed the least percent error for the estimation of $\Delta\beta/\beta$ across various incidence angles. ([[2014-kola|Kola, 2014, p. 73]])
- The condition numbers for the inversion of $\Delta\rho/\rho$ were very high, leading to a high percentage of error (e.g., values like 0.1163 ± 0.190 in Model 2). ([[2014-kola|Kola, 2014, p. 71]])
- Estimation of parameters from TPP amplitude fitting was found to be quite good for obtaining $\Delta\alpha/\alpha$ and $\Delta\rho/\rho$. ([[2014-kola|Kola, 2014, p. 77]])

## Limitations

The study notes that estimation based on receiver gathers is only applicable to homogeneous media and may not be suitable for cases involving lateral inhomogeneity or anisotropy. Additionally, the specific error parameters used in Model 1, 2, and 3 are unique to this study's models and cannot be generalized.

## Recommendations and Future Work

- Conduct more modeling studies to obtain representative theoretical curves for offset-angle transformation.
- Explore other expansion methods for approximating and linearizing Zoeppritz equations to improve accuracy at higher angles of incidence.
- Develop a common transmission point (CTP) sorting method to account for lateral heterogeneity and anisotropy, similar to CDP in reflection AVO.
- Extend the methodology to multi-layer models using synthetic data from FWM software and test on real VSP data.
